question: At its core, what type of on-chain game is Cosmic Signature?

options.label: A
options.text: A prediction market where players stake ETH on outcomes of future events.
options.rationale: Wrong — the game involves no external events or outcome prediction; players only compete over bid timing.

options.label: B
options.text: A last-bidder-wins auction where a countdown timer is refreshed by each bid.
options.rationale: Correct — a bid makes you the "last bidder" and extends the countdown; the last bidder at expiry wins.

options.label: C
options.text: A yield-farming vault that pays interest on deposited ETH and CST.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no passive yield on held ETH or CST; rewards come from actively bidding or staking.

options.label: D
options.text: A marketplace that matches CST buyers and sellers automatically.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no built-in exchange inside the game; CST trades, if any, happen elsewhere.

explanation: Cosmic Signature is a last-bidder-wins game. Every bid pushes the main-prize countdown further into the future; when the timer finally runs out, whoever placed the most recent bid is entitled to claim the main prize.

question: Which blockchain network is Cosmic Signature designed to run on?

options.label: A
options.text: Ethereum mainnet, to benefit from the largest validator set.
options.rationale: Wrong — mainnet fees would make the many small bids impractical, and the random-number logic relies on Arbitrum.

options.label: B
options.text: Polygon, for near-zero transaction fees.
options.rationale: Wrong — Polygon is a different network; the game is not deployed there.

options.label: C
options.text: Arbitrum, a layer-2 network that settles to Ethereum.
options.rationale: Correct — the game lives on Arbitrum.

options.label: D
options.text: Optimism, another popular layer-2 network.
options.rationale: Wrong — Optimism is a different layer-2; the game is not there.

explanation: The game is deployed on Arbitrum and uses features of that network to generate its random numbers on-chain.

question: A player can place a bid using which two currencies?

options.label: A
options.text: ETH only, with or without an attached Random Walk NFT.
options.rationale: Wrong — ETH is one bid type, but CST is a fully separate bid type.

options.label: B
options.text: ETH or the Cosmic Signature Token (CST).
options.rationale: Correct — ETH and CST are the two bid currencies accepted by the game.

options.label: C
options.text: USDC or ETH, depending on the round.
options.rationale: Wrong — USDC is not supported.

options.label: D
options.text: CST or the Cosmic Signature NFT (COSMIC).
options.rationale: Wrong — the Cosmic Signature NFT is a prize, never a bid medium.

explanation: There are two bid types: an ETH bid and a CST bid. ETH bids can optionally include a Random Walk NFT for a discount, but that is a modifier — not a separate currency.

question: What ends a bidding round?

options.label: A
options.text: A fixed clock expires exactly 24 hours after the round starts, regardless of bids.
options.rationale: Wrong — the deadline is extended by every new bid, so it is not a fixed clock.

options.label: B
options.text: Someone burns enough CST to trigger an early close.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST burns happen as part of CST bids, but they do not close the round.

options.label: C
options.text: A user successfully claims the main prize after the countdown has elapsed.
options.rationale: Correct — the main-prize claim distributes prizes and advances the round counter.

options.label: D
options.text: The operator of the game manually closes the round at their discretion.
options.rationale: Wrong — the operator cannot force-close a round; closure happens via a user's claim.

explanation: A round only ends when someone claims the main prize. That claim can only happen after the main-prize countdown has finished.

question: What is the ticker symbol of the Cosmic Signature Token?

options.label: A
options.text: COSIG
options.rationale: Wrong — COSIG is not the configured symbol.

options.label: B
options.text: CSG
options.rationale: Wrong — CSG is not the configured symbol.

options.label: C
options.text: CST
options.rationale: Correct — CST is the ticker of the Cosmic Signature Token.

options.label: D
options.text: COSMIC
options.rationale: Wrong — COSMIC is the ticker of the Cosmic Signature NFT, not the token.

explanation: The Cosmic Signature Token's ticker is CST. COSMIC is the separate ticker used by the Cosmic Signature NFT.

question: What is the ticker symbol of the Cosmic Signature NFT?

options.label: A
options.text: CST
options.rationale: Wrong — CST is the fungible token, not the NFT.

options.label: B
options.text: COSMIC
options.rationale: Correct — the NFT uses the COSMIC symbol.

options.label: C
options.text: CSNFT
options.rationale: Wrong — CSNFT is not used.

options.label: D
options.text: SIG
options.rationale: Wrong — SIG is not used anywhere in the game.

explanation: The NFT's symbol is COSMIC, distinct from the token's CST ticker.

question: Which role is awarded to the player who remained the last bidder for the longest single uninterrupted stretch of time in a round?

options.label: A
options.text: Chrono-Warrior
options.rationale: Wrong — the Chrono-Warrior recognizes holding the Endurance Champion title, not the raw last-bidder streak.

options.label: B
options.text: Endurance Champion
options.rationale: Correct — the Endurance Champion has the longest single continuous last-bidder interval.

options.label: C
options.text: Last CST Bidder
options.rationale: Wrong — that role goes to whoever most recently bid with CST, independent of duration.

options.label: D
options.text: Main Prize Winner
options.rationale: Wrong — the Main Prize Winner is simply whoever successfully claims the main prize.

explanation: The Endurance Champion is the person whose single continuous run as last bidder is the longest of the round.

question: Who receives the main ETH prize when a round ends?

options.label: A
options.text: The Endurance Champion, automatically sent at round end.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Endurance Champion gets CST and a Cosmic Signature NFT, not the main ETH prize.

options.label: B
options.text: The Chrono-Warrior, as part of the Chrono-Warrior prize bundle.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Chrono-Warrior receives a separate smaller ETH prize, not the main prize.

options.label: C
options.text: Whoever successfully claims the main prize — normally the last bidder, or anyone after a timeout.
options.rationale: Correct — the main prize goes to whoever successfully claims it.

options.label: D
options.text: A randomly drawn bidder selected during the prize distribution.
options.rationale: Wrong — random draws distribute smaller raffle prizes, not the main prize.

explanation: The main prize is paid directly to whoever calls the claim action. Only the last bidder can claim during the exclusive window; after a timeout, anyone can.

question: What are the two tokens minted by the Cosmic Signature game itself?

options.label: A
options.text: CST and RWLK
options.rationale: Wrong — RWLK is a third-party NFT; the game cannot mint it.

options.label: B
options.text: CST and the Cosmic Signature NFT (COSMIC)
options.rationale: Correct — CST and COSMIC are the two tokens the game mints.

options.label: C
options.text: RWLK and COSMIC
options.rationale: Wrong — the game never mints RWLK.

options.label: D
options.text: ETH and CST
options.rationale: Wrong — ETH is received and distributed, not minted.

explanation: The game is the sole source of newly minted CST and newly minted COSMIC NFTs. Everything else flows in from outside.

question: What is the purpose of the Charity Wallet?

options.label: A
options.text: To hold CST that will later fund marketing efforts.
options.rationale: Wrong — that is the role of the Marketing Wallet, which holds CST.

options.label: B
options.text: To collect a percentage of each round's ETH balance, to be sent to charity.
options.rationale: Correct — the Charity Wallet receives ETH earmarked for charity.

options.label: C
options.text: To lock bidder deposits until the next round begins.
options.rationale: Wrong — bidder deposits immediately become part of the game's prize pool.

options.label: D
options.text: To pay gas rebates to users who place many bids.
options.rationale: Wrong — the game does not refund gas to frequent bidders.

explanation: At the end of each round, a set percentage of the game's ETH balance is transferred toward charity. The Charity Wallet holds that ETH until someone forwards it to the chosen charity.

question: What is the purpose of the Marketing Wallet?

options.label: A
options.text: To reward ETH to bidders who promote the project on social media.
options.rationale: Wrong — the wallet holds CST, not ETH.

options.label: B
options.text: To accept third-party NFT donations during a round.
options.rationale: Wrong — donated NFTs go to the Prizes Wallet instead.

options.label: C
options.text: To hold the CST supply used to fund marketing initiatives.
options.rationale: Correct — it is CST-denominated and funds the marketing program.

options.label: D
options.text: To manage the treasury's ETH reserves.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no ETH treasury managed by this wallet.

explanation: At the end of every round, a fixed amount of new CST (3,000 CST by default) is minted to the Marketing Wallet. A treasurer appointed by the operator distributes it to fund marketing work.

question: How are governance decisions made for the Cosmic Signature ecosystem?

options.label: A
options.text: A multi-signature wallet controlled by the founding team signs each change.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no multi-sig governance layer.

options.label: B
options.text: A DAO where voting power is based on CST holdings votes on proposals.
options.rationale: Correct — CST holders vote in the DAO.

options.label: C
options.text: Off-chain polls that the operator executes manually.
options.rationale: Wrong — governance happens on-chain, not on external polls.

options.label: D
options.text: The operator decides alone, with no community governance.
options.rationale: Wrong — the operator has administrative powers, but a DAO exists on top.

explanation: There is a dedicated DAO whose voting power comes from how much CST each voter has delegated. Holders propose and vote on changes.

question: What restriction applies to the very first bid of every new round?

options.label: A
options.text: It must be placed using CST to prove CST liquidity exists.
options.rationale: Wrong — a CST first bid is specifically refused.

options.label: B
options.text: It must be placed using ETH.
options.rationale: Correct — the first bid must be ETH.

options.label: C
options.text: It must come from a Random Walk NFT holder.
options.rationale: Wrong — Random Walk NFTs are optional on ETH bids; they do not constrain the first bid.

options.label: D
options.text: There is no restriction; any bid type opens the round.
options.rationale: Wrong — the first-bid-must-be-ETH rule is strictly enforced.

explanation: The first bid of every round is required to be ETH; the game refuses a CST bid when no one has bid yet. An ETH first bid also kicks off the CST pricing clock for the round.

question: What happens to the round's countdown each time a new bid is placed?

options.label: A
options.text: The deadline is pushed further into the future.
options.rationale: Correct — every bid pushes the deadline later.

options.label: B
options.text: The deadline is shortened to create urgency.
options.rationale: Wrong — the countdown is never shortened by a bid.

options.label: C
options.text: The deadline resets only on CST bids; ETH bids leave it unchanged.
options.rationale: Wrong — both bid types extend the deadline equally.

options.label: D
options.text: The deadline is frozen while a bid is being processed.
options.rationale: Wrong — the deadline is updated in one step; nothing is "frozen".

explanation: Every bid extends the main-prize deadline; the countdown never moves backward.

question: What role does the Prizes Wallet play in the system?

options.label: A
options.text: It stores ETH donations until the next round's activation.
options.rationale: Wrong — ETH donations go straight into the game's own balance.

options.label: B
options.text: It safekeeps secondary ETH prizes, donated tokens, and donated NFTs until winners withdraw them.
options.rationale: Correct — the Prizes Wallet custodies secondary and donated prizes.

options.label: C
options.text: It manages the CST supply available for DAO proposals.
options.rationale: Wrong — the DAO reads CST balances directly, not from this wallet.

options.label: D
options.text: It runs the Dutch auction mechanism for CST.
options.rationale: Wrong — Dutch auctions run inside the main game, not in this wallet.

explanation: The Prizes Wallet holds secondary ETH prizes, donated tokens, and donated NFTs on behalf of winners. Winners can withdraw any time before a 5-week timeout, after which anyone can sweep them.

question: How many stages does a bidding round pass through?

options.label: A
options.text: Two: open and finished.
options.rationale: Wrong — this misses the "active before any bid" stage, which is functionally distinct.

options.label: B
options.text: Three: inactive, active before any bid, and active after the first bid.
options.rationale: Correct — inactive, active-no-bid, active-with-bid.

options.label: C
options.text: Four: pending, open, closing, and archived.
options.rationale: Wrong — the game distinguishes three stages, not four.

options.label: D
options.text: One: a round is always live until someone wins it.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is a deliberate delay between rounds, so a round is not always live.

explanation: A round is inactive until its activation time, then active-with-no-bids until the first bid arrives, then active-with-bids from that point on.

question: Who is permitted to mint new CST tokens?

options.label: A
options.text: Any CST holder, on demand.
options.rationale: Wrong — holders can only burn their own tokens, never mint.

options.label: B
options.text: The operator, via an admin function.
options.rationale: Wrong — the operator cannot mint directly; minting is automatic in the game's flow.

options.label: C
options.text: Only the Cosmic Signature game itself, as part of bid rewards and round payouts.
options.rationale: Correct — only the game creates new CST.

options.label: D
options.text: The Marketing Wallet, when it runs low.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Marketing Wallet receives newly minted CST but does not mint it itself.

explanation: Only the Cosmic Signature game itself is permitted to create new CST. Regular holders can only burn their own balance.

question: Can the Cosmic Signature game be upgraded after deployment?

options.label: A
options.text: No, it is fully immutable once deployed.
options.rationale: Wrong — the game is designed to be upgradeable.

options.label: B
options.text: Yes, but only by the operator while the current round is idle between sessions.
options.rationale: Correct — only the operator, and only between rounds, can upgrade.

options.label: C
options.text: Yes, by anyone at any time through a public upgrade action.
options.rationale: Wrong — only the operator can trigger upgrades.

options.label: D
options.text: Only through a DAO vote during an active round.
options.rationale: Wrong — the DAO does not have upgrade authority on its own, and upgrades cannot happen mid-round.

explanation: The operator can push an upgrade, but only while the game is between rounds — never during an active round — which preserves trust mid-round.

question: Roughly what discount does attaching a Random Walk NFT give to an ETH bid?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — the discount is much larger than 10%.

options.label: B
options.rationale: Wrong — the discount is half, not a quarter.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Correct — about 50% off the ETH price.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — 90% is far too generous.

explanation: Presenting a Random Walk NFT cuts the ETH you need to pay roughly in half.

question: What does a bidder receive simply for placing a bid (before any round-end prizes)?

options.label: A
options.text: A small amount of newly minted CST, given for every bid regardless of type.
options.rationale: Correct — each bid mints CST to the bidder as an immediate reward.

options.label: B
options.text: A guaranteed Cosmic Signature NFT per bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — NFTs are minted only at round end to specific winners and raffle picks.

options.label: C
options.text: A free Random Walk discount on their next ETH bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Random Walk discount requires owning and presenting such an NFT; it is not a reward.

options.label: D
options.text: Nothing; rewards only arrive at round end.
options.rationale: Wrong — the CST reward is minted in the same transaction as the bid.

explanation: Every bid — ETH or CST — causes the game to mint a small CST reward (100 CST by default) directly to the bidder.

question: The Chrono-Warrior earns which prize at round end?

options.label: A
options.text: A share of the main ETH prize proportional to their streak.
options.rationale: Wrong — the main prize is separate and goes to the main-prize claimer.

options.label: B
options.text: An ETH prize equal to 8% of the game's balance, plus CST and a Cosmic Signature NFT.
options.rationale: Correct — 8% ETH plus CST and an NFT.

options.label: C
options.text: Only CST, with no ETH.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST alone describes the Endurance Champion, not the Chrono-Warrior.

options.label: D
options.text: Nothing; the role is purely honorary.
options.rationale: Wrong — the role carries a real ETH prize.

explanation: The Chrono-Warrior receives 8% of the game's ETH balance, a 1,000-CST payout, and one newly minted Cosmic Signature NFT.

question: Which round-end role receives CST and a Cosmic Signature NFT but no ETH?

options.label: A
options.text: Endurance Champion
options.rationale: Correct — CST and NFT, no ETH.

options.label: B
options.text: Chrono-Warrior
options.rationale: Wrong — the Chrono-Warrior also gets an ETH prize.

options.label: C
options.text: Main Prize Winner
options.rationale: Wrong — the Main Prize Winner gets the main ETH prize plus CST and an NFT.

options.label: D
options.text: The Marketing Wallet
options.rationale: Wrong — the Marketing Wallet receives CST, but no NFT.

explanation: The Endurance Champion's prize bundle is 1,000 CST plus one newly minted Cosmic Signature NFT — no ETH.

question: Roughly what percentage of the game's ETH balance is awarded as the main prize at round end?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — the main prize is much larger than 5%.

options.label: B
options.rationale: Correct — 25% by default.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Wrong — 50% is the sum of all round-end ETH payouts, not the main prize alone.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — the entire balance is never paid out in one round; about half carries over.

explanation: Exactly 25% of the game's ETH balance at claim time is paid out as the main prize.

question: Approximately what percentage of the game's ETH balance goes to charity each round?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — the charity share is larger than 1%.

options.label: B
options.rationale: Correct — 7% by default.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Wrong — 25% is the main prize.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — only about half of the balance is distributed each round.

explanation: 7% of the balance is sent toward charity each round by default.

question: How many secondary ETH raffle prizes are drawn for random bidders at round end?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — more than one ETH raffle winner is drawn per round.

options.label: B
options.rationale: Correct — three ETH raffle winners per round.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Wrong — 10 is the NFT raffle count, not the ETH raffle count.

options.label: D
options.text: One per bidder in the round
options.rationale: Wrong — the number is fixed at 3, independent of how many bidders there are.

explanation: Three random bidders each receive an equal share of the ETH raffle pool.

question: What type of token is the Cosmic Signature NFT (COSMIC)?

options.label: A
options.text: A standard fungible currency-style token.
options.rationale: Wrong — that describes CST, not the NFT.

options.label: B
options.text: A unique collectible, with each one individually distinguishable.
options.rationale: Correct — the NFT is a unique collectible.

options.label: C
options.text: A multi-token used for in-game items.
options.rationale: Wrong — each NFT is a single item, not a multi-token.

options.label: D
options.text: A stablecoin pegged to the dollar.
options.rationale: Wrong — the NFT has no peg.

explanation: Each Cosmic Signature NFT is unique; they are collectibles, not interchangeable units.

question: What type of token is CST?

options.label: A
options.text: A unique non-fungible token.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST is fungible; COSMIC is the non-fungible counterpart.

options.label: B
options.text: A multi-token used for achievements.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST is a single fungible token.

options.label: C
options.text: A standard fungible token with a built-in governance-voting extension.
options.rationale: Correct — a fungible token usable for governance.

options.label: D
options.text: A wrapped ETH derivative.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST is not tied to ETH's price.

explanation: CST is a regular fungible token — any unit is interchangeable with any other — and it also carries governance voting power.

question: Which wallet do staked Cosmic Signature NFTs live in?

options.label: A
options.text: The Prizes Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Prizes Wallet holds prizes, not staked NFTs.

options.label: B
options.text: The dedicated Cosmic Signature NFT staking wallet.
options.rationale: Correct — the dedicated staking wallet holds each staked Cosmic Signature NFT.

options.label: C
options.text: The Marketing Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Marketing Wallet only holds CST.

options.label: D
options.text: The game's main program itself.
options.rationale: Wrong — the main game does not custody staked NFTs.

explanation: Staked Cosmic Signature NFTs are transferred to and custodied by the dedicated staking wallet for the duration of the stake.

question: What kind of reward do Cosmic Signature NFT stakers earn?

options.label: A
options.text: A share of the ETH deposited to the staking wallet at each round's end.
options.rationale: Correct — stakers split the ETH deposited at each round's end.

options.label: B
options.text: A fixed monthly CST stipend.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no CST stipend; rewards are in ETH.

options.label: C
options.text: Guaranteed wins in every raffle draw.
options.rationale: Wrong — raffle picks are random; staking does not guarantee them.

options.label: D
options.text: A free ETH bid every round.
options.rationale: Wrong — bids always cost the current price; staking grants no bid subsidy.

explanation: At every round's end, a portion of the game's ETH is deposited into the staking wallet and split across every currently staked Cosmic Signature NFT.

question: Which role is only awarded when at least one CST bid was placed in the round?

options.label: A
options.text: Chrono-Warrior
options.rationale: Wrong — the Chrono-Warrior exists in every round that had any bids.

options.label: B
options.text: Last CST Bidder
options.rationale: Correct — the Last CST Bidder role is exclusive to rounds with a CST bid.

options.label: C
options.text: Endurance Champion
options.rationale: Wrong — the Endurance Champion is awarded regardless of CST bids.

options.label: D
options.text: Main Prize Winner
options.rationale: Wrong — the Main Prize Winner is determined by the claim, not by CST bids.

explanation: The Last CST Bidder prize — one Cosmic Signature NFT and 1,000 CST — is only awarded if at least one CST bid happened in the round. Otherwise that prize slot is simply skipped.

question: What is the hardcoded first-ever ETH bid price in round zero?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — an order of magnitude too small.

options.label: B
options.rationale: Correct — 0.0001 ETH.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Wrong — ten times too high.

options.label: D
options.text: 0.01 ETH
options.rationale: Wrong — a hundred times too high.

explanation: The very first bid of the very first round costs exactly 0.0001 ETH. This starting price is locked in at deployment and cannot be changed.

question: In how many rounds can a specific Random Walk NFT be used to bid?

options.label: A
options.text: Once ever across the lifetime of the game.
options.rationale: Correct — each Random Walk NFT can be used for a bid only once, ever.

options.label: B
options.text: Once per round, for unlimited rounds.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no per-round reset.

options.label: C
options.text: As long as the owner holds it.
options.rationale: Wrong — the restriction follows the NFT itself, not its owner.

options.label: D
options.text: Only in the first three rounds.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no round-count limit; the limit is absolute.

explanation: Each Random Walk NFT carries a single lifetime "bid ticket" — once it has been used for a bid, the game remembers that forever.

question: What is the primary benefit of staking a Random Walk NFT?

options.label: A
options.text: A share of the ETH that the game deposits each round.
options.rationale: Wrong — Random Walk staking pays no ETH rewards.

options.label: B
options.text: Eligibility for a round-end raffle that awards newly minted Cosmic Signature NFTs and CST to random Random Walk stakers.
options.rationale: Correct — the sole benefit is raffle eligibility.

options.label: C
options.text: The ability to bid with that Random Walk NFT for free.
options.rationale: Wrong — staking does not affect bidding cost.

options.label: D
options.text: Voting rights in the Cosmic Signature DAO.
options.rationale: Wrong — DAO voting comes from holding CST, not from staking.

explanation: Staking a Random Walk NFT has no ETH payout. Its only benefit is entering the round-end raffle that draws 10 random stakers, each receiving a Cosmic Signature NFT and 1,000 CST.

question: At round end, how is the Cosmic Signature NFT staker ETH reward distributed?

options.label: A
options.text: Entirely to the single largest staker.
options.rationale: Wrong — rewards are split per NFT, not concentrated.

options.label: B
options.text: Split equally across every currently staked Cosmic Signature NFT.
options.rationale: Correct — an equal per-NFT share.

options.label: C
options.text: Proportionally to how long each NFT has been staked.
options.rationale: Wrong — tenure does not affect the current-round share; every currently staked NFT gets the same slice.

options.label: D
options.text: To a single randomly drawn staker.
options.rationale: Wrong — all staked NFTs share; there is no single-winner pick.

explanation: The staking ETH deposit is divided equally across every Cosmic Signature NFT that is staked at that moment.

question: Who assigns the Marketing Wallet's treasurer?

options.label: A
options.text: The DAO, via a governance vote.
options.rationale: Wrong — the DAO only picks the treasurer if it has been given ownership of the wallet.

options.label: B
options.text: The wallet's owner (initially the deployer).
options.rationale: Correct — the owner appoints the treasurer.

options.label: C
options.text: Any CST holder via a direct transaction.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST holders have no direct access to this setting.

options.label: D
options.text: The Endurance Champion of the most recent round.
options.rationale: Wrong — round roles have no bearing on wallet administration.

explanation: The Marketing Wallet has its own owner role. That owner picks the treasurer who is allowed to send CST out of the wallet.

question: What happens to CST when it is used to pay for a CST bid?

options.label: A
options.text: It is transferred to the Marketing Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — the paid CST is burned, not routed to Marketing.

options.label: B
options.text: It is permanently destroyed (burned) from the bidder's balance.
options.rationale: Correct — the paid CST is destroyed.

options.label: C
options.text: It is transferred to the Charity Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Charity Wallet is ETH-only.

options.label: D
options.text: It is locked until the round ends.
options.rationale: Wrong — the CST is destroyed, not merely locked.

explanation: A CST bid burns the paid amount of CST forever. The bidder also receives a small CST reward for bidding, but the paid price itself is destroyed.

question: After the main-prize deadline is reached, who is initially allowed to claim the main prize?

options.label: A
options.text: Any bidder from the current round.
options.rationale: Wrong — non-last-bidders must wait for the grace period before being allowed to claim.

options.label: B
options.text: Only the last bidder.
options.rationale: Correct — the last bidder has an exclusive early-claim window.

options.label: C
options.text: Only the DAO, via proposal.
options.rationale: Wrong — the DAO has no special role in claiming.

options.label: D
options.text: The game's operator.
options.rationale: Wrong — the operator does not automatically get the prize.

explanation: Once the deadline elapses, the last bidder has an exclusive window to claim. Only after a further grace period (one day by default) does anyone else become eligible.

question: How many random bidders receive a Cosmic Signature NFT plus CST bundle at round end?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — three is the ETH raffle count, not the NFT raffle count.

options.label: B
options.rationale: Wrong — the NFT raffle count is 10.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Correct — ten random bidders each receive an NFT and 1,000 CST.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — the pool is 10, not 100.

explanation: Ten random bidders are drawn; each winner receives one newly minted Cosmic Signature NFT and 1,000 CST.

question: How are the winners of the round-end bidder raffles selected?

options.label: A
options.text: Based on the largest bid amount, ranked by ETH spent.
options.rationale: Wrong — the draw is random, not based on size spent.

options.label: B
options.text: Randomly from all bids placed in that round.
options.rationale: Correct — winners are random picks from every bid placed in the round.

options.label: C
options.text: The first ten bidders of the round.
options.rationale: Wrong — bidding order does not decide raffle winners.

options.label: D
options.text: The last ten bidders of the round.
options.rationale: Wrong — bidding last does not guarantee a raffle win.

explanation: Each raffle draw picks a random bid from the full list of bids placed in the round. Bidding more often gives you more tickets in the draw.

question: Can the same address win multiple raffle prizes in a single round?

options.label: A
options.text: No — the game explicitly deduplicates winners per round.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no deduplication between draws.

options.label: B
options.text: Yes — each raffle draw is independent, so a frequent bidder can be drawn multiple times.
options.rationale: Correct — independent draws, so multiple wins are possible.

options.label: C
options.text: Only if they placed more than 50 bids.
options.rationale: Wrong — no threshold applies; even one bid keeps you eligible.

options.label: D
options.text: Only if they are the Chrono-Warrior.
options.rationale: Wrong — Chrono-Warrior status does not change raffle eligibility.

explanation: Each raffle pick is independent. If you placed many bids, you have a better chance on every draw, and you can win more than one prize in a single round.

question: The Last CST Bidder role is awarded only when:

options.label: A
options.text: At least one CST bid was placed in the round.
options.rationale: Correct — the role exists only if someone bid with CST.

options.label: B
options.text: The last bidder overall paid with CST.
options.rationale: Wrong — the last bid overall can be ETH; the Last CST Bidder is just the most recent CST bidder.

options.label: C
options.text: The CST auction reached its floor price.
options.rationale: Wrong — the auction floor has no effect on this role.

options.label: D
options.text: The DAO approves awarding the role.
options.rationale: Wrong — DAO approval is not involved.

explanation: The role only exists when someone bid with CST in the round. If no CST bid ever happened, the prize slot is simply skipped.

question: What prize bundle does the Last CST Bidder receive?

options.label: A
options.text: One Cosmic Signature NFT and 1,000 CST.
options.rationale: Correct — one NFT and 1,000 CST.

options.label: B
options.text: Ten Cosmic Signature NFTs.
options.rationale: Wrong — only one NFT is awarded for this role.

options.label: C
options.text: 25% of the ETH balance.
options.rationale: Wrong — ETH prizes go to other roles.

options.label: D
options.text: Nothing; the role is symbolic.
options.rationale: Wrong — a real on-chain bundle is awarded.

explanation: The Last CST Bidder is awarded one newly minted Cosmic Signature NFT and 1,000 CST. No ETH is attached.

question: Where does an ETH donation made via the dedicated donation action go?

options.label: A
options.text: Directly to the Charity Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — the donation is not earmarked specifically for charity.

options.label: B
options.text: Into the game's own ETH balance, where it will be shared across all future winners.
options.rationale: Correct — donated ETH enters the game's balance and is shared.

options.label: C
options.text: Into the donor's personal Prizes Wallet balance for later withdrawal.
options.rationale: Wrong — donors do not receive personal prize balances.

options.label: D
options.text: Back to the donor at the end of the round.
options.rationale: Wrong — donations are not refunded.

explanation: Donations simply add to the game's ETH balance. Because round-end payouts are percentages of that balance, the donation ends up benefiting current and future winners.

question: Does a pure ETH donation extend the main-prize countdown?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, the same as a bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — only real bids extend the timer.

options.label: B
options.text: No, a donation is not a bid and does not affect the timer.
options.rationale: Correct — donations leave the timer untouched.

options.label: C
options.text: Yes, but only if at least 1 ETH is donated.
options.rationale: Wrong — no size threshold changes the rule.

options.label: D
options.text: Only during the first 24 hours of a round.
options.rationale: Wrong — donations never affect the timer, regardless of timing.

explanation: Donations are intentionally separate from bids. A donation credits ETH to the game but never makes the donor the last bidder and never shifts the deadline.

question: Which statement about CST bidding best matches the game's design?

options.label: A
options.text: CST bids are refunded if the bidder does not win.
options.rationale: Wrong — burning is permanent; nothing is refunded.

options.label: B
options.text: CST bids permanently destroy (burn) the paid CST from the bidder's balance.
options.rationale: Correct — the paid CST is burned.

options.label: C
options.text: CST bids require a minimum balance of 10,000 CST.
options.rationale: Wrong — no hardcoded minimum balance applies beyond covering the current price.

options.label: D
options.text: CST bids replace the last bidder's ETH bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — ETH and CST bids coexist; a CST bid does not erase an earlier ETH bid.

explanation: The paid CST is burned — destroyed for good. There is no refund path.

question: The game lengthens the between-bid timer increment slightly each round. Why?

options.label: A
options.text: To make the price bids cost more each round.
options.rationale: Wrong — bid-price growth is a separate mechanism.

options.label: B
options.text: To gradually stretch out how long a typical round takes.
options.rationale: Correct — future rounds get slightly longer by design.

options.label: C
options.text: To increase the CST prize amount.
options.rationale: Wrong — the CST prize amount does not change automatically.

options.label: D
options.text: To raise the charity percentage.
options.rationale: Wrong — the charity percentage is a static setting.

explanation: The increment added to the deadline on each bid grows by about 1% at the end of every round, so future rounds can run a touch longer.

question: What is the default delay between one round ending and the next becoming active?

options.label: A
options.text: Zero — the next round activates immediately.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is a deliberate delay.

options.label: B
options.text: 30 minutes.
options.rationale: Correct — 30 minutes.

options.label: C
options.text: 24 hours.
options.rationale: Wrong — the delay is minutes, not a day.

options.label: D
options.text: One week.
options.rationale: Wrong — the delay is minutes, not a week.

explanation: There is a deliberate half-hour gap between a round's main-prize claim and the next round becoming active, which lets the operator re-tune parameters if needed.

question: Which of the following is a prize awarded to the Marketing Wallet at each round's end?

options.label: A
options.text: 3,000 newly minted CST.
options.rationale: Correct — 3,000 CST minted per round.

options.label: B
options.text: Ten Cosmic Signature NFTs.
options.rationale: Wrong — the wallet receives no NFTs.

options.label: C
options.text: 7% of the game's ETH balance.
options.rationale: Wrong — the charity donation is ETH; the Marketing Wallet never receives ETH.

options.label: D
options.text: Nothing; the wallet is funded manually.
options.rationale: Wrong — funding is automatic.

explanation: The game mints 3,000 CST to the Marketing Wallet at each round's end to fund ongoing marketing activities.

question: The bid message attached to a bid is limited to at most how many characters?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — far shorter than the actual cap.

options.label: B
options.rationale: Wrong — that is the old Twitter limit.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Correct — 280 characters.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — longer than the actual cap.

explanation: Bid messages are capped at 280 characters, similar to the classic short-post format on social media. Longer messages are rejected.

question: Who is solely responsible for deciding what charity receives the Charity Wallet's ETH?

options.label: A
options.text: The main Cosmic Signature game.
options.rationale: Wrong — the main game doesn't decide the final charity recipient.

options.label: B
options.text: The DAO, which sets the wallet's charity address.
options.rationale: Wrong — the DAO only picks the charity if it is given ownership of the wallet.

options.label: C
options.text: The charity itself, by pulling funds.
options.rationale: Wrong — charities cannot pull funds; they have to wait to be sent ETH.

options.label: D
options.text: The Charity Wallet's owner (initially the deployer).
options.rationale: Correct — the Charity Wallet's owner sets the charity.

explanation: The Charity Wallet has its own owner, and only that owner can set the charity address. Ownership can later be transferred to the DAO, but the decision lives with whoever owns the wallet at any given moment.

question: After the first ETH bid of a round, each subsequent ETH bid's price is calculated as:

options.label: A
options.text: The prior bid price multiplied by two.
options.rationale: Wrong — doubling would be far too aggressive.

options.label: B
options.text: The prior bid price, raised by about 1% (plus the tiniest possible amount of ETH so the price always grows).
options.rationale: Correct — roughly 1% step per bid, plus a sliver.

options.label: C
options.text: A price set by the DAO each time a bid arrives.
options.rationale: Wrong — the DAO does not set bid prices.

options.label: D
options.text: A fixed amount per round, independent of the prior bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — prices change on every bid, not once per round.

explanation: Every same-round ETH bid costs about 1% more than the previous one — plus the smallest possible slice of ETH on top, to guarantee the price strictly grows even if the 1% rounds down to zero.

question: When you place an ETH bid with a Random Walk NFT, the paid price is calculated as:

options.label: A
options.text: Exactly half of the current ETH bid price, rounded down.
options.rationale: Wrong — the formula rounds up, not down.

options.label: B
options.text: The current ETH price divided by two, rounded up (a single extra unit when the price is odd).
options.rationale: Correct — half price, rounded up when the price is odd.

options.label: C
options.text: The full ETH price, with the NFT giving CST back instead of a discount.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Random Walk discount affects the ETH price directly; the usual CST bid reward is given separately.

options.label: D
options.text: Zero; the Random Walk NFT covers the entire price.
options.rationale: Wrong — the discount is not 100%.

explanation: The discount is half the current ETH price, rounded up by at most one indivisible unit of ETH. It is essentially a 50% discount, just never quite below exact half.

question: Which of these best describes how CST bid prices are formed inside a round?

options.label: A
options.text: A fixed starting price that never changes during the round.
options.rationale: Wrong — the price changes continuously and resets per CST bid.

options.label: B
options.text: A Dutch auction (price declining over time) that resets every time someone places a CST bid.
options.rationale: Correct — a Dutch auction that restarts after each CST bid.

options.label: C
options.text: A doubling auction that must be won within a fixed 10-minute window.
options.rationale: Wrong — the duration is much longer than 10 minutes by default.

options.label: D
options.text: A price based on the total supply of CST.
options.rationale: Wrong — the price is not derived from total supply.

explanation: CST bids use a Dutch auction: the price starts high and falls linearly over time. Each CST bid ends the current auction and starts a fresh one.

question: A round's first CST Dutch auction begins at what moment?

options.label: A
options.text: At the moment the game was originally deployed.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST auctions are round-scoped.

options.label: B
options.text: When the first ETH bid of the round is placed; then again after every CST bid.
options.rationale: Correct — the first ETH bid kicks off the first CST auction, and every CST bid resets the next one.

options.label: C
options.text: At the end of each round.
options.rationale: Wrong — auctions begin inside a round, not at round end.

options.label: D
options.text: Whenever the DAO approves it.
options.rationale: Wrong — the DAO does not gate CST auctions.

explanation: The round's first CST auction starts when the round's first ETH bid lands. After that, every CST bid starts a new auction.

question: If a round's CST Dutch auction runs past its end duration with no bid, what is the "current" CST bid price?

options.label: A
options.text: The full beginning price.
options.rationale: Wrong — the price declines, not stays put.

options.label: B
options.text: Zero.
options.rationale: Correct — zero once the duration elapses.

options.label: C
options.text: The configured CST starting-price floor.
options.rationale: Wrong — the floor applies only to the next auction's starting price, not the ongoing decline.

options.label: D
options.text: The price of the previous CST bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — the floor is zero, not the last paid price.

explanation: Once the auction's configured duration has fully elapsed with no CST bid, the current price reported by the game is zero. A bidder at that moment would pay 0 CST (though the bid still triggers all the other per-bid effects).

question: Who is recognized as the Endurance Champion if only one bidder ever bids in a round (no one outbids them)?

options.label: A
options.text: Nobody — the title is unassigned.
options.rationale: Wrong — the title is always assigned once there is a bid.

options.label: B
options.text: That single bidder — they held the last-bidder position for the entire round, which becomes their winning streak.
options.rationale: Correct — the sole bidder is the Endurance Champion.

options.label: C
options.text: The DAO, as a fallback.
options.rationale: Wrong — the DAO has no fallback role.

options.label: D
options.text: The last bidder of the previous round.
options.rationale: Wrong — previous-round roles do not carry over.

explanation: At claim time the game evaluates the current last-bidder as the Endurance Champion if nobody else ever took that title. A single bidder's run spans the whole round and wins automatically.

question: Can the Endurance Champion and the Chrono-Warrior be the same person in one round?

options.label: A
options.text: No — the game explicitly prevents it.
options.rationale: Wrong — overlap is common.

options.label: B
options.text: Yes — especially in rounds with few outbidding events, the same player often holds both titles.
options.rationale: Correct — a single bidder frequently holds both.

options.label: C
options.text: Only in round zero.
options.rationale: Wrong — nothing about this is round-specific.

options.label: D
options.text: Only if the player used a Random Walk NFT.
options.rationale: Wrong — Random Walk use is irrelevant to champion tracking.

explanation: The two titles can absolutely belong to the same person. When there is only one reign as Endurance Champion, the Chrono-Warrior is by definition that same person.

question: On a bid that is not the first of the round, which rule best describes how the deadline is extended?

options.label: A
options.text: The deadline becomes "now plus the timer-extension increment", ignoring the existing deadline.
options.rationale: Wrong — using only "now" would shorten the deadline when a bid lands before expiry.

options.label: B
options.text: The deadline becomes "whichever is later of the existing deadline and now, plus the timer-extension increment".
options.rationale: Correct — the later of the two points, plus the increment.

options.label: C
options.text: The deadline becomes "the existing deadline plus the increment", even if the existing deadline is already in the past.
options.rationale: Wrong — if the current deadline is already past, the game anchors to now, not to the stale value.

options.label: D
options.text: The deadline becomes "now plus twice the increment".
options.rationale: Wrong — the increment is added once, not doubled.

explanation: The deadline is anchored to the later of (a) the current deadline or (b) the current time, and then the increment is added. This prevents a late bid from accidentally shortening the round.

question: What initial duration until the main prize is granted on the first bid of a round, at default configuration?

options.label: A
options.text: About 5 minutes.
options.rationale: Wrong — the default is on the order of a day.

options.label: B
options.text: About 24 hours.
options.rationale: Correct — approximately 24 hours.

options.label: C
options.text: Exactly 7 days.
options.rationale: Wrong — longer than the default.

options.label: D
options.text: It varies every round and has no typical value.
options.rationale: Wrong — the default has a consistent target when settings are unchanged.

explanation: With default settings, the first bid sets a roughly day-long countdown until the main prize is first claimable.

question: Roughly what total percentage of the game's ETH balance is paid out each round across all ETH prizes?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — that is only the main prize alone.

options.label: B
options.rationale: Correct — about half of the balance is paid out each round.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Wrong — overstates the payout share.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — the design deliberately rolls half over.

explanation: Summing the defaults: 25% (main prize) + 8% (Chrono-Warrior) + 4% (bidder raffle) + 6% (NFT stakers) + 7% (charity) = 50%. The other 50% rolls over to the next round.

question: What does the Cosmic Signature DAO use to measure voting power on a proposal?

options.label: A
options.text: ETH balance.
options.rationale: Wrong — ETH confers no voting power.

options.label: B
options.text: The voter's CST voting weight at the moment the proposal's snapshot was taken.
options.rationale: Correct — snapshot CST voting weight decides the vote.

options.label: C
options.text: The number of bids placed in the previous round.
options.rationale: Wrong — bid history is not used.

options.label: D
options.text: The number of Cosmic Signature NFTs held.
options.rationale: Wrong — Cosmic Signature NFTs have no voting weight.

explanation: DAO votes are weighted by how much CST voting power each voter has when the proposal was snapshotted. CST holdings power governance.

question: What is the default share of CST that must back a DAO proposal for it to reach quorum?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — below the configured value.

options.label: B
options.rationale: Correct — 3% of CST supply at snapshot.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Wrong — far higher than the configured default.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — 51% is not the quorum threshold.

explanation: The DAO intentionally uses a low 3% quorum to make it easier for proposals to reach the threshold.

question: What is the default DAO voting period (the window in which votes can be cast on a proposal)?

options.label: A
options.text: 1 hour.
options.rationale: Wrong — much longer than an hour.

options.label: B
options.text: 2 days.
options.rationale: Wrong — 2 days is the voting *delay* (the wait before voting opens), not the voting period.

options.label: C
options.text: 2 weeks.
options.rationale: Correct — 2 weeks.

options.label: D
options.text: 30 days.
options.rationale: Wrong — less than a month.

explanation: Voters have about two weeks to cast their votes after a proposal's voting window opens.

question: What is the default DAO voting delay (the wait between proposal creation and voting opening)?

options.label: A
options.text: Zero — voting opens immediately.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is a deliberate delay.

options.label: B
options.text: 2 days.
options.rationale: Correct — 2 days.

options.label: C
options.text: 2 weeks.
options.rationale: Wrong — 2 weeks is the voting period, not the delay.

options.label: D
options.text: 6 months.
options.rationale: Wrong — unrealistically long.

explanation: After a proposal is created there is a 2-day wait before voting can actually begin, giving voters time to read it.

question: What is the default proposal threshold — the minimum CST a submitter needs to create a DAO proposal?

options.label: A
options.text: 1 CST.
options.rationale: Wrong — the threshold is higher than one token.

options.label: B
options.text: 100 CST.
options.rationale: Correct — 100 CST.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Wrong — that is the per-winner CST prize, not the proposal threshold.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — far higher than the real threshold.

explanation: Submitting a proposal requires at least 100 CST of voting weight. That figure matches the per-bid CST reward, so any bidder who participated a bit has enough to propose.

question: On a successful main-prize claim, which ETH transfer is attempted last and, if it fails, will reverse the whole claim?

options.label: A
options.text: The transfer to charity.
options.rationale: Wrong — a failed charity transfer is logged and ignored.

options.label: B
options.text: The direct transfer of the main prize to the claimer.
options.rationale: Correct — a failed main-prize transfer reverses the whole claim.

options.label: C
options.text: The deposit to Cosmic Signature NFT stakers.
options.rationale: Wrong — the staker deposit happens earlier and has its own safety net.

options.label: D
options.text: The raffle deposit to the Prizes Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — raffle deposits happen earlier in the flow.

explanation: If the main-prize transfer to the claimer fails, the entire claim is rolled back. The charity transfer, by contrast, is allowed to fail silently so the round still closes.

question: What happens if the charity transfer fails during the round-end payout?

options.label: A
options.text: The entire claim is reversed, and the prize goes unclaimed.
options.rationale: Wrong — the claim does not reverse on a charity failure.

options.label: B
options.text: The failure is logged, the ETH stays in the game, and the remaining prizes still distribute.
options.rationale: Correct — the failure is non-fatal; the payout continues.

options.label: C
options.text: The charity ETH is rolled into the main prize.
options.rationale: Wrong — the failed charity ETH simply stays in the game; it is not redirected to the main prize.

options.label: D
options.text: The charity address is automatically cleared.
options.rationale: Wrong — no auto-clear of the charity address.

explanation: The game deliberately tolerates a failed charity transfer: it logs an event, keeps the charity ETH in the game's balance, and carries on with the rest of the payout.

question: If the Cosmic Signature NFT staking deposit cannot happen because there are no stakers (you cannot divide by zero), what happens?

options.label: A
options.text: The round's claim is aborted.
options.rationale: Wrong — the claim completes gracefully.

options.label: B
options.text: The game catches the problem safely; the 6% of ETH earmarked for stakers simply stays in the game for the next round.
options.rationale: Correct — the staker ETH stays in the game and carries over.

options.label: C
options.text: The ETH is forwarded to charity instead.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no redirect to charity.

options.label: D
options.text: The ETH is destroyed.
options.rationale: Wrong — the ETH is not destroyed.

explanation: The game watches for exactly this case. When the staker count is zero, it absorbs the problem, keeps the ETH, and lets the rest of the round-end payout complete.

question: For how long after a round's end can a prize winner exclusively withdraw their ETH from the Prizes Wallet?

options.label: A
options.text: 1 day.
options.rationale: Wrong — the exclusivity window is weeks, not a day.

options.label: B
options.text: 1 week.
options.rationale: Wrong — 5 weeks is the default, not 1.

options.label: C
options.text: 5 weeks.
options.rationale: Correct — 5 weeks of exclusivity.

options.label: D
options.text: Forever.
options.rationale: Wrong — exclusivity ends at 5 weeks.

explanation: Winners have a 5-week exclusive withdrawal window. Once that elapses, anyone else can sweep an unclaimed prize.

question: After the Prizes Wallet withdrawal timeout, who may withdraw an unclaimed ETH prize?

options.label: A
options.text: Only the winner, still; anyone else fails.
options.rationale: Wrong — exclusivity ends at the timeout.

options.label: B
options.text: Anyone; the unclaimed balance is sent to whoever calls the withdraw action.
options.rationale: Correct — any caller can sweep the unclaimed prize to themselves.

options.label: C
options.text: Only the Charity Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Charity Wallet has no special claim.

options.label: D
options.text: Only the operator.
options.rationale: Wrong — the operator has no built-in claim privilege.

explanation: After the 5-week timeout, any address can call the withdraw action for a stale prize, and the ETH is paid to the caller — not to the original winner.

question: What is the maximum number of characters a bid message may contain by default?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — far shorter than the actual cap.

options.label: B
options.rationale: Wrong — the cap is 280, not 140.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Correct — 280 characters.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — much larger than the actual cap.

explanation: Bid messages are capped at 280 characters, the classic short-post size. Longer messages are rejected.

question: At the end of a round, the next round's first CST Dutch auction beginning price equals:

options.label: A
options.text: Zero — CST always starts free.
options.rationale: Wrong — the floor keeps the starting price above zero.

options.label: B
options.text: Twice the first CST bid price of the current round (not below the configured floor), or unchanged if no CST bid occurred.
options.rationale: Correct — twice the first CST paid price, clamped to the floor, or unchanged without a CST bid.

options.label: C
options.text: The Marketing Wallet's current CST balance.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Marketing Wallet plays no role in CST pricing.

options.label: D
options.text: The main prize amount converted at a market rate.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no market-rate conversion.

explanation: When the round's first CST bid lands, the game notes "double that price, clamped to the floor" as the starting price for next round's first CST auction. If no CST bid ever happened, this value is untouched.

question: Can the same Random Walk NFT be used for a bid twice across two different rounds?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes — the "used" flag resets each round.
options.rationale: Wrong — no per-round reset.

options.label: B
options.text: No — once it has been used for a bid, it is marked forever and cannot be used again.
options.rationale: Correct — the flag persists forever.

options.label: C
options.text: Only if the NFT is transferred to a new owner.
options.rationale: Wrong — transferring does not clear the flag.

options.label: D
options.text: Only if the owner stakes it in between.
options.rationale: Wrong — staking and bidding are tracked separately, but the bid-use flag is permanent.

explanation: A Random Walk NFT's bid-use flag is permanent. Even transferring it to someone else does not reset the flag.

question: Can a Random Walk NFT that has already been used for a bid still be staked later?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes — the bid-use mark and the stake-use mark are separate tracks.
options.rationale: Correct — the two marks are separate; staking is still allowed.

options.label: B
options.text: No — the game treats them as the same flag.
options.rationale: Wrong — the two lists are independent.

options.label: C
options.text: Only after a 24-hour cool-down.
options.rationale: Wrong — no cool-down exists.

options.label: D
options.text: Only if the owner burns 1,000 CST first.
options.rationale: Wrong — no CST fee is involved.

explanation: The game keeps two independent "used" lists: one for bid-uses, one for stake-uses. Being used for a bid does not prevent the NFT from being staked.

question: Once a Cosmic Signature NFT has been unstaked, can it be staked again?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, immediately.
options.rationale: Wrong — re-staking is blocked.

options.label: B
options.text: No — each Cosmic Signature NFT can only be staked once in its lifetime.
options.rationale: Correct — one stake, ever.

options.label: C
options.text: Yes, but only after one round passes.
options.rationale: Wrong — no time-based reset.

options.label: D
options.text: Yes, but the new owner has to burn 100 CST.
options.rationale: Wrong — no unlock via burn.

explanation: Each Cosmic Signature NFT has a one-stake lifetime cap. Once it has been unstaked, the game refuses any further stake attempt for that NFT.

question: When you unstake a Cosmic Signature NFT, how are your accumulated ETH rewards transferred?

options.label: A
options.text: You must call a separate claim action afterward.
options.rationale: Wrong — no separate claim is required.

options.label: B
options.text: The NFT and the accrued ETH come back to you in the same transaction.
options.rationale: Correct — NFT and ETH come out together.

options.label: C
options.text: The ETH is routed to the Prizes Wallet first and must be withdrawn from there.
options.rationale: Wrong — rewards are delivered directly.

options.label: D
options.text: The ETH is converted to CST and sent to you.
options.rationale: Wrong — rewards are paid in ETH, not CST.

explanation: Unstaking both returns your NFT and pays out your accrued ETH rewards in a single transaction.

question: If you stake multiple Cosmic Signature NFTs at different times, how do their rewards accrue?

options.label: A
options.text: All staked NFTs earn the same amount regardless of when they were staked.
options.rationale: Wrong — earlier-staked NFTs may have collected more past deposits.

options.label: B
options.text: Each NFT accumulates independently from deposits made while it was staked.
options.rationale: Correct — each NFT earns only from deposits made during its own stake window.

options.label: C
options.text: Only the most recently staked NFT earns rewards.
options.rationale: Wrong — every currently staked NFT earns.

options.label: D
options.text: Rewards are pooled and divided equally at the end of the year.
options.rationale: Wrong — rewards are not annual.

explanation: Each NFT remembers the per-NFT total reward level at the moment it was staked. On unstake, you collect the difference — so each NFT only captures what came in during its own time staked.

question: The Cosmic Signature NFT staker reward accrual formula on each round-end deposit is:

options.label: A
options.text: The entire deposit is added to every staked NFT.
options.rationale: Wrong — each NFT gets a share, not the full deposit.

options.label: B
options.text: The deposit is split equally across every currently staked NFT.
options.rationale: Correct — equal per-NFT share.

options.label: C
options.text: The deposit is split by NFT id.
options.rationale: Wrong — NFT id is not a weighting factor.

options.label: D
options.text: The deposit is split by how much CST each staker holds.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST balances do not factor into staking rewards.

explanation: Every currently staked Cosmic Signature NFT gets the same per-NFT share of each deposit.

question: Which of these can donate a standard fungible token into the prize pool along with a bid?

options.label: A
options.text: Any bidder, using the special "bid with donation" variants of either ETH or CST bids.
options.rationale: Correct — any bidder can attach a token donation to a bid.

options.label: B
options.text: Only the DAO.
options.rationale: Wrong — no DAO-only path exists.

options.label: C
options.text: Only the operator.
options.rationale: Wrong — the operator has no special privilege here.

options.label: D
options.text: Only the Marketing Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Marketing Wallet is not a donor.

explanation: Both ETH and CST bid entry points have companion variants that let a bidder attach a third-party token donation in the same transaction.

question: Who can claim donated standard tokens from a particular round?

options.label: A
options.text: The CST holder with the highest balance.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST holdings do not gate the claim.

options.label: B
options.text: The main prize winner of that round, or — after the withdrawal timeout — anyone.
options.rationale: Correct — round winner during the window; anyone after timeout.

options.label: C
options.text: The DAO.
options.rationale: Wrong — the DAO has no special claim.

options.label: D
options.text: The Random Walk NFT holders.
options.rationale: Wrong — Random Walk NFT holders have no special claim.

explanation: Inside the timeout window, only the round's main prize winner can claim donated tokens. After the 5-week timeout, anyone may claim them.

question: When a standard token is donated to a round, where does it physically land?

options.label: A
options.text: Directly in the Prizes Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Prizes Wallet coordinates but does not hold the tokens directly.

options.label: B
options.text: In a per-round "donated-token holder" helper created on the first donation of that round.
options.rationale: Correct — a per-round helper holds donated tokens.

options.label: C
options.text: In the main game.
options.rationale: Wrong — the main game never holds donated tokens.

options.label: D
options.text: Destroyed (burned) to a dead address.
options.rationale: Wrong — tokens are not burned on donation.

explanation: Donated tokens of any given round are custodied by a per-round helper that the Prizes Wallet creates on the first donation of that round.

question: What does the "donate ETH with info" action do beyond a plain donation?

options.label: A
options.text: Rejects the call if the sender has no CST.
options.rationale: Wrong — no CST requirement.

options.label: B
options.text: Records the donor, round number, amount, and a small message on-chain for later reference.
options.rationale: Correct — a detailed record is stored alongside the donation.

options.label: C
options.text: Doubles the donation's impact on the timer.
options.rationale: Wrong — donations never affect the timer.

options.label: D
options.text: Destroys the donated ETH.
options.rationale: Wrong — the ETH is retained, not destroyed.

explanation: "Donate with info" adds the same ETH as a plain donation, but also stores a message tagged with who donated, when, and how much. It is still not a bid.

question: What happens when someone simply sends raw ETH to the main game (no specific action, just a plain transfer)?

options.label: A
options.text: It is treated as an ETH bid with no attached Random Walk NFT and no message.
options.rationale: Correct — it is a plain ETH bid.

options.label: B
options.text: It is treated as a donation with no info.
options.rationale: Wrong — donations require the dedicated donation action.

options.label: C
options.text: It always fails.
options.rationale: Wrong — only fails if the bid itself cannot go through.

options.label: D
options.text: It is redirected to the Prizes Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — no redirect exists.

explanation: Plain ETH transfers to the main game enter the ETH bid path as if the sender had called the ETH-bid action with no options. If the amount is not enough for the current price or the round is not active, the transaction fails.

question: Which of the following settings can be changed even while the round is live (as long as no bid has been placed yet)?

options.label: A
options.text: The charity percentage.
options.rationale: Wrong — the charity percentage requires a fully idle round to change.

options.label: B
options.text: The delay before the next round activates.
options.rationale: Wrong — the between-round delay has its own rule; it is not the looser one described here.

options.label: C
options.text: The round's activation time.
options.rationale: Correct — the activation time's guard is specifically "no bid yet has been placed".

options.label: D
options.text: The starting page address for Cosmic Signature NFT metadata.
options.rationale: Wrong — that setting lives on the separate NFT side of the system with different rules.

explanation: Most settings can only be changed while a round is idle between sessions, but the round's activation time has a looser rule: as long as no one has bid yet, the operator can shift it.

question: Roughly how much ETH stays in the game's balance after a typical round ends, on default parameters?

options.label: A
options.rationale: Wrong — about half carries over.

options.label: B
options.text: About 50%
options.rationale: Correct — roughly half the balance persists.

options.label: C
options.text: About 75%
options.rationale: Wrong — payout is closer to 50%, not 25%.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — payout happens each round.

explanation: Default percentages pay out about 50% of the balance each round (main prize 25, Chrono-Warrior 8, raffle 4, stakers 6, charity 7). The remaining 50% seeds the next round.

question: On the first bid of a non-zero round, the ETH Dutch auction begins at what price relative to the previous round?

options.label: A
options.text: The same first-bid price as the previous round.
options.rationale: Wrong — the starting price doubles each round by default.

options.label: B
options.text: Twice the first-bid price of the previous round.
options.rationale: Correct — starts at twice the previous first-bid price.

options.label: C
options.text: Half the first-bid price of the previous round.
options.rationale: Wrong — halving is an emergency lever, not the standard starting rule.

options.label: D
options.text: Zero — it always starts from zero.
options.rationale: Wrong — round zero starts at a hardcoded nonzero; other rounds at double.

explanation: At the start of every new round (except the very first), the Dutch auction begins at double the first-bid price paid in the previous round.

question: What is the ETH Dutch auction's ending (floor) price on a given round?

options.label: A
options.text: Always 0 ETH.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is always a tiny nonzero floor.

options.label: B
options.text: A small fraction of the beginning price (with a tiny nudge so it cannot be exactly zero).
options.rationale: Correct — a small fraction of the beginning, with a tiny nudge above zero.

options.label: C
options.text: 0.0001 ETH in every round.
options.rationale: Wrong — 0.0001 ETH is only the hardcoded round-zero first-bid price.

options.label: D
options.text: Exactly twice the beginning price.
options.rationale: Wrong — the floor is below, not above, the beginning.

explanation: The floor is the beginning price divided by a configured number, plus a tiny extra amount so it is always strictly positive. With defaults, the floor is roughly 1/200 of the beginning price.

question: In which situation can the ETH bid price become exactly the Dutch auction's ending (floor) price?

options.label: A
options.text: Only immediately after a claim.
options.rationale: Wrong — the floor is reached via waiting, not by post-claim state.

options.label: B
options.text: Whenever the auction's configured duration has fully elapsed with nobody bidding.
options.rationale: Correct — the floor pins once the duration is up.

options.label: C
options.text: Only when the operator pulls the emergency halving lever.
options.rationale: Wrong — that lever lowers the floor further, but the floor was already reachable.

options.label: D
options.text: Never — the floor is purely theoretical.
options.rationale: Wrong — reaching the floor is explicitly supported.

explanation: The price falls to the floor once the auction duration has fully elapsed without a bid. The emergency lever lets the operator push it even lower, but reaching the normal floor just requires waiting out the auction.

question: Which role, if assigned, always receives exactly one Cosmic Signature NFT and no ETH beyond that NFT?

options.label: A
options.text: Main Prize Winner.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Main Prize Winner also receives the main ETH prize.

options.label: B
options.text: Endurance Champion.
options.rationale: Correct — no ETH for the Endurance Champion.

options.label: C
options.text: Chrono-Warrior.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Chrono-Warrior gets 8% ETH in addition to the NFT.

options.label: D
options.text: Bidder raffle NFT winners.
options.rationale: Wrong — that describes a group of winners, not a single one.

explanation: The Endurance Champion always receives one Cosmic Signature NFT and 1,000 CST, with no ETH attached. The Main Prize Winner and the Chrono-Warrior both get ETH on top of their NFT.

question: What happens when someone tries to bid with ETH but sends less than the current price?

options.label: A
options.text: The game accepts the funds and records a partial bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — partial bids are not supported.

options.label: B
options.text: The transaction fails and the funds are not taken.
options.rationale: Correct — the transaction fails.

options.label: C
options.text: The game treats the call as a donation instead.
options.rationale: Wrong — only the dedicated donation action triggers donation behavior.

options.label: D
options.text: The game auto-tops-up the shortfall from the Prizes Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — no auto top-up exists.

explanation: If the ETH sent is below the required price, the game rejects the bid. No partial bids exist.

question: What is the net CST change for a bidder who places a CST bid that pays a price p?

options.label: A
options.text: Their balance drops by p and nothing else.
options.rationale: Wrong — the 100 CST reward is still minted.

options.label: B
options.text: Their balance drops by p and then rises by the per-bid CST reward (100 CST by default).
options.rationale: Correct — burn p, mint 100, net is -p + 100.

options.label: C
options.text: Their balance rises by p as a reward.
options.rationale: Wrong — the price is never returned as a reward.

options.label: D
options.text: Their balance is unaffected.
options.rationale: Wrong — bids always change the bidder's CST balance unless price exactly equals 100.

explanation: Every bid, including a CST bid, mints a 100-CST reward to the bidder. So the net change from a CST bid is "minus price paid, plus 100".

question: When is the Chrono-Warrior state updated during the round?

options.label: A
options.text: On every bid, including the first.
options.rationale: Wrong — the first bid does not trigger the update.

options.label: B
options.text: On every bid after the first, and once more on the main-prize claim.
options.rationale: Correct — non-first bids and the final claim drive updates.

options.label: C
options.text: Only on CST bids.
options.rationale: Wrong — both bid types trigger updates.

options.label: D
options.text: Only at round-end, never during bidding.
options.rationale: Wrong — updates happen during the round, not only at the end.

explanation: The Chrono-Warrior is updated on every non-first bid inside the round, and then once more at claim time to finalize the standings.

question: If nobody bids for the entire Dutch auction duration in round 1, and then someone bids at the floor price, what happens to the next round's beginning ETH price?

options.label: A
options.text: It becomes 0 because the floor bid was tiny.
options.rationale: Wrong — doubling the floor gives a small nonzero price.

options.label: B
options.text: It becomes twice the floor price of round 1 — very small, so round 2 starts cheap.
options.rationale: Correct — next round's start is 2× the floor price.

options.label: C
options.text: It resets to 0.0001 ETH like round zero.
options.rationale: Wrong — round zero's hardcoded price is not re-applied.

options.label: D
options.text: It becomes the price at which the Dutch auction started.
options.rationale: Wrong — the starting value is tied to the paid price, not to the auction's original starting value.

explanation: The next round's starting price is always twice the first bid price of the previous round. If that first bid was at the floor, the next round also starts very cheaply.

question: What happens to the CST Dutch auction state when a new CST bid is placed?

options.label: A
options.text: Nothing — the auction keeps ticking unchanged.
options.rationale: Wrong — the auction explicitly restarts.

options.label: B
options.text: The start-time and start-price are reset, and a fresh declining auction begins.
options.rationale: Correct — the timestamp and starting price both reset.

options.label: C
options.text: The auction pauses until the next ETH bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST auctions are not gated on ETH bids after the round's first ETH bid.

options.label: D
options.text: The auction finishes and the CST bidder earns a special prize.
options.rationale: Wrong — CST bids do not award a special prize.

explanation: Every CST bid ends the current auction and begins a fresh one: the start-time is reset to now, and the start-price is set to twice the paid price (clamped to the floor).

question: When is the carry-over CST starting price for the next round's first CST auction actually written?

options.label: A
options.text: On every CST bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — only the first CST bid writes it.

options.label: B
options.text: Only on the first CST bid of the round; any later CST bids do not change it.
options.rationale: Correct — only the round's first CST bid.

options.label: C
options.text: On every ETH bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — ETH bids do not touch this value.

options.label: D
options.text: Only at round end.
options.rationale: Wrong — it is set mid-round, not at end.

explanation: The value that seeds the next round's first CST auction is saved only when the round's first CST bid arrives. Subsequent CST bids in the same round do not change it.

question: How are DAO voting timings measured?

options.label: A
options.text: In block numbers.
options.rationale: Wrong — the project explicitly opts out of block-based timing.

options.label: B
options.text: In seconds (a real-time clock).
options.rationale: Correct — timing is in real-time seconds.

options.label: C
options.text: In rounds of the game.
options.rationale: Wrong — rounds are a game concept, unrelated to DAO timing.

options.label: D
options.text: There is no clock; timing is manual.
options.rationale: Wrong — timing is automatic and on-chain.

explanation: CST and the DAO are configured to run on a real-time seconds clock, so voting delays and voting periods are measured in seconds, not in blocks.

question: How many Cosmic Signature NFTs can a given address receive from the round-end bidder NFT raffle?

options.label: A
options.text: At most one.
options.rationale: Wrong — draws are independent.

options.label: B
options.text: As many as the number of times it is randomly drawn (possibly more than once).
options.rationale: Correct — a frequent bidder can win multiple raffle NFTs.

options.label: C
options.text: Exactly one per CST bid placed.
options.rationale: Wrong — the raffle is independent of bid type or count.

options.label: D
options.text: Proportional to ETH spent.
options.rationale: Wrong — ETH spend is not the weighting.

explanation: Each of the 10 raffle draws is independent. A bidder with many bids can get drawn multiple times in the same round.

question: When a CST Dutch auction's time has fully elapsed (no bid during the window), what price does the game report?

options.label: A
options.text: A roll-over price from the previous round.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no roll-over from prior rounds in this calculation.

options.label: B
options.text: Zero.
options.rationale: Correct — the current price is zero.

options.label: C
options.text: The configured starting-price floor.
options.rationale: Wrong — the floor clamps the next auction's starting price, not the ongoing decline.

options.label: D
options.text: The maximum possible value (and the transaction fails).
options.rationale: Wrong — no failure; the price is simply zero.
