explanation: The Prizes Wallet stores donated NFTs with claim rules similar to donated tokens: the round's main prize winner can claim them during the timeout window; after the timeout, anyone can.

question: Suppose the DAO is given ownership of the main game. Once done, what changes?

options.label: A
options.text: Nothing — DAO ownership is the same as deployer ownership.
options.rationale: Wrong — ownership transfer means the DAO now acts through its own vote flows.

options.label: B
options.text: The DAO gains the same administrative privileges the operator had, under the same round-state rules (for example, upgrades still require an idle round).
options.rationale: Correct — the DAO inherits the same powers and guards.

options.label: C
options.text: Users can no longer bid.
options.rationale: Wrong — user bidding is unaffected.

options.label: D
options.text: The Charity Wallet is also automatically governed by the DAO.
options.rationale: Wrong — the Charity Wallet has its own owner.

explanation: Handing the game's ownership to the DAO preserves all existing rules — the DAO gets the same administrative powers the operator had, subject to the same round-state guards.

question: Which of the following is TRUE about the claim-after-timeout flow?

options.label: A
options.text: After the deadline plus the grace period, any caller can claim the main prize and receive it; the last bidder loses their exclusive right.
options.rationale: Correct — anyone can claim after the grace period.

options.label: B
options.text: The DAO must approve each late claim.
options.rationale: Wrong — no governance gate.

options.label: C
options.text: Late claimers must pay a fee to the Marketing Wallet.
options.rationale: Wrong — no fee mechanism.

options.label: D
options.text: Late claimers forfeit the secondary prizes.
options.rationale: Wrong — the claimer still receives the main-prize bundle.

explanation: Once the grace period is up, any caller is permitted. The main prize goes to the caller, regardless of whether they bid.

question: Default percentages are: main 25%, Chrono-Warrior 8%, raffle 4%, stakers 6%, charity 7% — totaling 50%. If there are zero Cosmic Signature NFT stakers in a given round, what fraction of the game's balance is actually paid out?

options.label: A
options.text: 50%, unchanged.
options.rationale: Wrong — the staker share is effectively skipped.

options.label: B
options.text: 44% — the 6% staker deposit is safely skipped and that ETH stays in the game.
options.rationale: Correct — 44% leaves.

options.label: C
options.rationale: Wrong — many prizes still pay.

options.label: D
options.rationale: Wrong — does not match the formula.

explanation: With no stakers, the staker deposit is skipped and the 6% stays. The other 25 + 8 + 4 + 7 = 44% does leave the game.

question: Which of these is FALSE about the Prizes Wallet?

options.label: A
options.text: ETH balances are tracked per winner per round.
options.rationale: True — tracked per winner per round.

options.label: B
options.text: Donated tokens are custodied by a per-round helper component.
options.rationale: True — a helper holds donated tokens.

options.label: C
options.text: Donated collectibles are held by the Prizes Wallet itself.
options.rationale: True — donated NFTs are held by the Prizes Wallet directly.

options.label: D
options.text: Cosmic Signature NFT staking rewards are held here.
options.rationale: Correct answer — this claim is FALSE.

explanation: Cosmic Signature NFT staking rewards live in the dedicated staking wallet, not in the Prizes Wallet. The other three statements are true.

question: In round 10: Alice 50 bids, Bob 50 bids, Charlie 1 bid. The raffle picks 10 NFT winners. What is the approximate probability Charlie wins at least one?

options.label: A
options.text: About 0.99%.
options.rationale: Wrong — that is a single-draw probability.

options.label: B
options.text: About 9.6% — 1 minus (100/101) to the 10th power.
options.rationale: Correct — about 9.6%.

options.label: C
options.text: Exactly 1%.
options.rationale: Wrong — 10 draws boost the probability above 1%.

options.label: D
options.text: About 50%.
options.rationale: Wrong — far too high.

explanation: Charlie's per-draw chance is 1/101. Miss-all probability across 10 draws is (100/101) to the 10th ≈ 0.905, so win-at-least-one ≈ 0.095.

question: The ETH bid price grows by about 1% per bid. A bid at price P followed by exactly 100 more bids produces what new price, approximately?

options.label: A
options.text: P plus a tiny 100-unit nudge.
options.rationale: Wrong — the tiny additive slice is negligible compared to compounding.

options.label: B
options.text: About P × e, where e ≈ 2.718.
options.rationale: Correct — 1% compounded 100 times approaches e.

options.label: C
options.text: Exactly 2P.
options.rationale: Wrong — 1.01 to the 100 is about 2.705, very close to e.

options.label: D
options.text: A price approaching infinity.
options.rationale: Wrong — 100 bids is finite.

explanation: (1 + 1/100) compounded 100 times approaches the mathematical constant e ≈ 2.718.

question: Why does the game enforce "the ETH you send must be greater than zero" only when no bid has been placed in the round yet?

options.label: A
options.text: To require that the first bid of each round is ETH.
options.rationale: Partial — correct but incomplete.

options.label: B
options.text: To allow CST bids after the first bid without forcing them to send ETH.
options.rationale: Partial — correct but incomplete.

options.label: C
options.text: Both A and B — the single check serves as both the first-bid-must-be-ETH rule and the permission for later CST bids to skip ETH.
options.rationale: Correct — the check serves both purposes in one line.

options.label: D
options.text: Neither — it is a vestigial check.
options.rationale: Wrong — the check is essential.

explanation: That one check both enforces "first bid must be ETH" and allows later CST bids (which of course send no ETH) to proceed.

question: What is the primary reason the champion-tracking step runs on every non-first bid and also on the main-prize claim?

options.label: A
options.text: To keep the Endurance Champion and Chrono-Warrior accurate through every transition and at the final evaluation moment.
options.rationale: Correct — accuracy at every transition plus finalization.

options.label: B
options.text: To prevent front-running.
options.rationale: Wrong — unrelated.

options.label: C
options.text: To reward bidders with CST.
options.rationale: Wrong — the CST reward is a separate step.

options.label: D
options.text: To refresh the random-number generator.
options.rationale: Wrong — the random-number seed comes from other helpers.

explanation: Champions are computed incrementally. Running the step on every non-first bid catches each transition; running it once more on claim captures the final reign up to the claim time.

question: An attacker force-sends ETH to the main game in a way that does not call any specific action (for example, by destroying a different wallet-program that held the ETH). What happens?

options.label: A
options.text: The ETH simply credits the game's balance, with no bid and no event logged.
options.rationale: Correct — the balance increases silently.

options.label: B
options.text: The game rejects the incoming ETH.
options.rationale: Wrong — there is no way to reject a force-send.

options.label: C
options.text: A raffle is triggered.
options.rationale: Wrong — no raffle is triggered.

options.label: D
options.text: The ETH is returned to the sender automatically.
options.rationale: Wrong — no automatic bounce-back exists.

explanation: Force-sending ETH this way bypasses the game's normal receive path entirely. The game's balance goes up, but no bid runs and no donation is logged.

question: A round closes with the game holding a balance of 40 ETH, 200 bids from 50 bidders, 10 staked Cosmic Signature NFTs, 0 staked Random Walk NFTs, and a Last CST Bidder, Endurance Champion, and Chrono-Warrior. Under default parameters, approximately how much ETH stays in the game after all payouts complete?

options.label: A
options.text: 0 ETH.
options.rationale: Wrong — half carries over by design.

options.label: B
options.text: About 20 ETH (50% remains).
options.rationale: Correct — about 20 ETH remains after the 50% payout.

options.label: C
options.text: 40 ETH — nothing is distributed.
options.rationale: Wrong — distribution does happen.

options.label: D
options.text: About 10 ETH.
options.rationale: Wrong — does not match the defaults.

explanation: Defaults pay out about 50%: main 10 + charity 2.8 + Chrono-Warrior 3.2 + raffle 1.6 + stakers 2.4 = 20 ETH paid out; 20 ETH remains. No Random Walk stakers does not change ETH flows — only the NFT-and-CST raffle bundles for that category are skipped.
